exhibitions.title: Sculpting Time: Video Installations 1990–Now
exhibitions.description: A chronological journey through ambitious video environments that stretch, loop, and fracture time.

articles.title: Kusama on Screen: Digital Dots Take Over the Facade
articles.subtitle: Projection-mapped polka dots announce the arrival of Yayoi Kusama’s Infinity Patterns
articles.excerpt: The museum’s facade becomes a temporary canvas for Yayoi Kusama’s signature dots in a new digital commission.
articles.body: As the city’s winter light fades, Yayoi Kusama’s luminous dots spill across the museum’s facade in a new projection commission tied to "Yayoi Kusama: Infinity Patterns." The large-scale intervention extends Kusama’s vocabulary into urban space, synchronizing with the mirrored rooms inside. Technicians spent weeks mapping every architectural seam to ensure the dots drift seamlessly around corners and windows, transforming the building into a dynamic, shifting painting each evening.

articles.title: Behind the Scenes: Lighting Kusama’s New Infinity Room
articles.subtitle: How a tiny team choreographs reflections for millions of selfies
articles.excerpt: Lighting designers reveal the meticulous testing behind the newest Yayoi Kusama infinity room.
articles.body: Days before opening, the newest infinity room for "Yayoi Kusama: Infinity Patterns" is still a construction site of cables, mirrored panels, and LED tests. The lighting team walks us through more than a dozen pre-programmed light sequences that will cycle throughout the exhibition’s run. Every change in color temperature shifts how visitors perceive depth, reflections, and even their own silhouettes in the room’s mirrored walls.

articles.title: Mirror Universes: Installing 'Yayoi Kusama: Infinity Patterns'
articles.subtitle: Crates, cranes, and countless dots arrive at the museum
articles.excerpt: From the first crate to the final mirror, a look at how Kusama’s immersive worlds arrive on site.
articles.body: Over several nights, trucks carrying Yayoi Kusama’s installations back up to the museum’s loading dock. Inside, registrars, art handlers, and conservators carefully uncrate mirrored panels, sculptures, and paintings that span decades of the artist’s practice. Each room is mapped out on the floor in tape before a single object is moved into place. "Infinity Patterns" will be one of the largest Kusama presentations in the region, and the installation pace reflects the exhibition’s ambitious scale.

articles.title: Tickets Announced for 'Yayoi Kusama: Infinity Patterns'
articles.subtitle: Timed entries, late nights, and member previews revealed
articles.excerpt: Plan your visit to Yayoi Kusama’s upcoming exhibition with new details on ticket releases and member access.
articles.body: The museum has announced timed-entry tickets for "Yayoi Kusama: Infinity Patterns," opening September 3, 2025. Member presales begin next week, with a limited number of free community days scheduled throughout the run. While general admission to the museum remains unchanged, the exhibition requires a separate, limited-capacity ticket to manage high demand for the infinity rooms.

articles.title: Rehearsing with Avatars: Inside 'Digital Choreographies'
articles.subtitle: Dancers, coders, and motion capture rigs share the same studio floor
articles.excerpt: Behind-the-scenes in the studio where performers rehearse alongside their digital doubles.
articles.body: For "Digital Choreographies: Moving Bodies in Code," dancers rehearse not only in front of mirrors but inside motion capture rigs. As performers run phrases, their movements are translated in real time into polygonal avatars that glitch, stretch, and multiply across projection screens. The exhibition explores how choreographers collaborate with coders to rethink the stage as a mutable, software-driven environment.

articles.title: When Algorithms Curate the Wall
articles.subtitle: A curatorial experiment lets code propose unexpected juxtapositions
articles.excerpt: What happens when curators debate with software over which works hang next to each other?
articles.body: For the exhibition "Code and Canvas," curators invited an in-house team of developers to build a simple recommendation engine that suggests wall layouts based on color, metadata, and visitor attention patterns. The final installation reflects a series of negotiations between human and machine preferences, revealing how algorithmic tools can both challenge and reinforce curatorial habits.

articles.title: Who Pays for the New Wing? Rethinking Ticket Prices
articles.subtitle: Why architecture, access, and funding can’t be separated
articles.excerpt: The museum’s new wing is stunning, but its impact will be measured through access as much as architecture.
articles.body: The opening of the new wing has revived an old debate: how should ticket prices reflect the true cost of maintaining ambitious contemporary art spaces? While donors underwrite much of the expansion, the pressure to raise revenue through admissions is real. This essay argues that ticket prices are not just a budget line but a curatorial decision that shapes who feels entitled to cross the threshold, linger in the atrium, and return regularly.

articles.title: Bodies in the Atrium: Performance as Architecture
articles.subtitle: January’s live works treat the museum as a responsive stage
articles.excerpt: A series of performance works turns staircases, balconies, and foyers into choreographed environments.
articles.body: Throughout January, performers repeatedly traverse the museum’s grand atrium, tracing invisible lines between balconies, staircases, and entry points. These works echo the building’s circulation patterns, revealing how visitors’ movements already form an ongoing choreography. Rather than treating the museum as a neutral container, the program frames architecture itself as a collaborator in performance.

articles.title: Review: 'Signals in Space' Turns Movement into Signal
articles.subtitle: Performance installations that listen as intently as they look
articles.excerpt: The exhibition "Signals in Space" asks what happens when performance leaves a data trail.
articles.body: "Signals in Space: Performance Installations" tracks how bodies trigger lights, sounds, and projections across the gallery. Visitors quickly realize that every step is being registered by sensors, cameras, and microphones. The show shines when it foregrounds this apparatus, making us newly aware of how our presence activates the work.

articles.title: Scoring the Everyday: January Performance Lab
articles.subtitle: Visitors turn daily gestures into performance scores
articles.excerpt: A month-long lab invites participants to write and perform scores based on routine actions.
articles.body: In the January Performance Lab, visitors experiment with writing simple performance scores based on everyday gestures—tying shoes, checking phones, standing in line. These scores are then interpreted by a rotating group of performers who treat the galleries as open rehearsal spaces, blurring the boundaries between workshop and exhibition.

articles.title: Review: 'Fragments of the City' Finds Poetry in Infrastructure
articles.subtitle: Overpasses, scaffolds, and subway grates become unlikely protagonists
articles.excerpt: The exhibition pulls urban fragments into the gallery, asking us to look again at the city’s overlooked surfaces.
articles.body: “Fragments of the City” assembles photographs, found objects, and projected video loops into a dense urban collage. The strongest works treat infrastructure as a kind of accidental public sculpture, revealing how scaffolds, grates, and jersey barriers choreograph our movements every day. With a modest ticket price and generous wall texts, the show feels unusually welcoming to first-time visitors.

articles.title: Review: 'Photography of Noise' Turns Listening into Looking
articles.subtitle: Grain, glitch, and static as visual music
articles.excerpt: A tightly edited exhibition treats the photograph as a recording of interference rather than clarity.
articles.body: “Photography of Noise” revels in the limits of lens-based media. Long exposures smear city lights into abstract compositions, while hacked cameras turn sensor errors into a new kind of visual rhythm. The show is most compelling when images and sound bleed together, as in a room where speakers play reamplified camera hiss.

articles.title: Review: 'Glass and Glow' Shines but Never Overheats
articles.subtitle: A cool-headed survey of luminous glass installations
articles.excerpt: Collaborations between glassblowers and lighting designers produce precise, if sometimes chilly, environments.
articles.body: “Glass and Glow” pairs fragile glass forms with carefully tuned LEDs, creating rooms that feel equal parts laboratory and chapel. The exhibition’s restraint is admirable, though some viewers may crave moments of riskier experimentation. Still, the show is an accessible introduction to how light can behave as a sculptural material.

articles.title: Review: 'Sonic Architectures' Makes the Museum Hum
articles.subtitle: Sound installations that treat the building as an instrument
articles.excerpt: From stairwells to elevators, this exhibition turns circulation spaces into resonant chambers.
articles.body: In “Sonic Architectures,” microphones and speakers hide in plain sight along railings and vents, rerouting ambient sound through subtle feedback loops. The most memorable piece layers live audio from the ticket hall with archival recordings of the building’s construction, creating a duet between past and present.

articles.title: Interview: Choreographing for the Game Engine
articles.subtitle: A choreographer and a game designer discuss rehearsing in virtual space
articles.excerpt: What changes when dancers rehearse inside motion capture rigs instead of mirrors?
articles.body: In this conversation, a choreographer and a game designer unpack the process behind their contribution to “Digital Choreographies.” They reflect on working with latency, avatar glitches, and the strange intimacy of watching one’s movements rendered as polygons in real time.

articles.title: Interview: The Sound of Empty Galleries
articles.subtitle: A sound artist on listening to spaces before the art arrives
articles.excerpt: Before installing a single speaker, the artist spends hours listening to vents, stairwells, and corners.
articles.body: The artist behind one of the most immersive works in “Sonic Architectures” describes the practice of listening to a gallery’s existing hum—the HVAC systems, distant traffic, and muffled conversations—before adding any new sound. For them, the piece begins with amplifying what’s already there.

articles.title: Interview: Reprogramming the Landscape
articles.subtitle: Simulated environments and the future of digital nature
articles.excerpt: An artist discusses building synthetic ecosystems for the exhibition “Digital Landscapes: Reprogrammed Nature.”
articles.body: Working with game engines and custom shaders, the artist crafts virtual forests that grow in response to live weather data. In this interview, they describe balancing realism and abstraction, and why digital nature should feel slightly uncanny rather than purely escapist.

articles.title: Interview: Archives That Perform Themselves
articles.subtitle: On scores, scripts, and reactivating performance histories
articles.excerpt: A curator of “Performance Legacies: Staging the Archive” explains how documents can behave like scripts.
articles.body: Rather than treating archival material as evidence of a finished event, the curator behind “Performance Legacies” frames scores, photographs, and notes as invitations for future restagings. In this conversation, they discuss the ethics of reactivating performances and working with artists’ estates.

articles.title: Interview: Building a Museum Without Walls
articles.subtitle: Imagining institutions that spill into streets and screens
articles.excerpt: An artist from “Museum Without Borders” reflects on working beyond the gallery’s physical limits.
articles.body: For “Museum Without Borders,” artists staged interventions in transit hubs, social media feeds, and civic meetings. Here, one participating artist describes how collaborating with neighborhood organizers reshaped their understanding of what a museum can be and whom it is for.

comments.body: I appreciate this nuanced look at how ticket prices shape who actually feels welcome in the new wing. Transparent budgeting, community days, and flexible pricing models seem essential if access is truly a priority.

The essay helped me understand how ticket prices, architecture, and donor expectations are linked. I would love to see more concrete examples of museums that lowered admission and still funded ambitious programs.
Thank you for highlighting how approachable this exhibition feels for first-time visitors. The focus on overlooked urban details really resonates with how I experience the city every day.
Seeing the facade transformed at night was incredible. It made the whole block feel like part of the Kusama show, even for people just passing by after work.
